text: Newport's case for "deep work" — the ability to concentrate without distraction on cognitively demanding tasks — rests on a straightforward economic argument: as automation and artificial intelligence commoditize routine cognitive labor, the capacity to master hard things quickly and produce at a high level becomes disproportionately valuable. Yet the same networked tools that enable knowledge workers to collaborate globally also fragment the attention those workers need for depth. The result is a professional culture that is simultaneously more connected and less capable of the concentrated effort that creates genuinely scarce value.
question: According to the passage, what economic dynamic makes deep work increasingly valuable?

options.text: Remote work has expanded the global talent pool, raising the premium on rare skills
Automation commoditizes routine cognitive labor, making the capacity for mastery and high output disproportionately scarce
Corporate investment in distraction-free workspaces has declined, making those who can self-regulate more productive
The attention economy has made shallow information so abundant that depth commands a price premium
